news.title: Why videos of Yves SakilaвАЩs last moments on Dublin street are key to Garda investigation
news.summary: On Friday at about 5pm, Yves Sakila(35) was pursued by security guards on DublinвАЩs Henry Street. He was suspected of shoplifting. As he ran down the street, it is believed he collided with an elderly man. What happened next was witnessed by several bystanders and by thousands of others because of a distressing video posted on social media. It shows the Congolese manвАЩs last minutes as he lay face down restrained by several security men. When garda√ђ arrived on the scene, Sakila had been held on the ground for at least five minutes. The injured man, in his 80s, was also on the ground a few metres away. Both were taken to hospital where Sakila was pronounced dead and the older man was operated on. So what happened? The incident is being investigated by both the Garda and by Fiosr√Ї, the police ombudsman. How will these investigations proceed? politics.title: The History Wars and America at 250, with the Historian Jill Lepore
politics.podcast_name: The Political Scene | The New Yorker
politics.summary: The two-hundred-and-fiftieth anniversary of the Declaration of Independence arrives during intense disputes about American history, as the Trump Administration demands a more glorifying view of the nationвАЩs past at federally run historical sites and in federally funded projects. The staff writer Jill Lepore (who won the Pulitzer Prize in History this month for her book вАЬWe the People: A History of the U.S. ConstitutionвАЭ) guest-hosts a special episode of The New Yorker Radio Hour about this fraught moment, reflecting on the responsibility of academic historians to shape the public debate. She compares our moment with the bicentennialвАФwhich fell in the wake of the Vietnam War and the scandals of Richard NixonвАЩs PresidencyвАФin a conversation with the Yale historian Beverly Gage. Lepore looks at the nature of the countryвАЩs war over history with Jelani Cobb, the dean of Columbia Journalism School and a staff writer at The New Yorker. They discuss the Donald Trump-approved вАЬFreedom 250вАЭ projection on the Washington Monument, and talk about how Americans can meaningfully participate in the semiquincentennial. If вАЬweвАЩre sitting around waiting for the occupant of the White House to tell us what American history means,вАЭ Lepore says, вАЬyou just kind of want to walk into traffic.вАЭ¬† Further reading:¬† America at 250, a special issue of The New Yorker вАЬWas the Declaration of Independence Better Before the Edits?,вАЭ by Jill Lepore вАЬScandal, Protest, Goofiness, and Grandeur at the U.S. Bicentennial,вАЭ by Jill Lepore вАЬTwo Hundred and Fifty Years of Complicated Commemorations,вАЭ by Jelani Cobb вАЬThis Land Is Your Land: A Road Trip Through U.S. History,вАЭ by Beverly Gage See the Washington Roundtable live at 92NY on June 4th. society_and_culture.title: JTG MINI: 'Should I Marry A Murderer?' The Netflix doco everyone is talking about.
society_and_culture.summary: Everyone seems to have an opinion on the new Netflix doco 'Should I Marry A Murderer?', including us, because we talked about it for so long in this week's bonus Breakin' Nooz episode that we decided it needed to be its own MINI ep. Just the gist: Dr Caroline Muirhead, a Glasgow forensic pathologist, met Sandy McKellar on Tinder, got engaged within weeks, then found out her new dream man had killed someone... and she didn't leave him. Two years earlier, Sandy and his twin brother Robert McKellar had killed Tony Parsons - a cancer survivor doing a charity bicycle ride. Sandy and Robert told Caroline it was a drunken hit and run and they panicked and hid the body. Caroline says she stayed with Sandy to try and collect information, because the police weren't helping her and she was terrified. But since the release of 'Should I Marry A Murderer?', opinion has been sharply divided on Caroline's actions, with people either calling her a hero trauma survivor or a selfish drug addict. But in a world where we expect perfect victims, is it even possible for the public to accept multiple things can be true at once? We give you Just The Gist, but if you want more, there's Breakin' Nooz, JTG After Show AND Bonus eps! society_and_culture.title: Is It Time to Break the Two-Party System?
society_and_culture.summary: We have entered a world of maximum gerrymandering warfare. Any guardrails that once existed, from the Constitution or the courts, have been bulldozed over the last decade вАУ most recently in the Supreme Court decision that gutted the Voting Rights Act and made it harder for minorities to challenge racially discriminatory voting maps. Red and blue states alike have been aggressively trying to redraw their congressional maps in response to all these developments. And there is no sign that will end in 2028; legislatures will just continue trying to tweak their lines to squeeze out advantage for whatever party is in power. And competitive districts in this country вАУ already an endangered species вАУ now teeter on extinction. That is, unless something dramatic changes. Lee Drutman is a senior fellow in the political reform program at New America. HeвАЩs one of the most persistent and thoughtful advocates of selecting House members through proportional representation вАУ a system used in many other countries that would make gerrymandering much more difficult. HeвАЩs the author of the 2020 book вАЬBreaking the Two-Party Doom Loop: The Case for Multiparty Democracy in AmericaвАЭ and writes the newsletter Undercurrent Events. arts.title: Friday Focus: No winners in the Middle East вАФ and Xi warns Trump against falling into the Thucydides Trap
arts.summary: Tickets to our May 20th Munk Debate on Foreign Wars taking place in Toronto and featuring Mike Pompeo, Victoria Nuland, John Mearsheimer and Stephen Walt, are almost sold out. Visit https://munkdebates.com/debates/foreign-wars-debate/ to purchase tickets.  Rudyard and Janice start todayвАЩs show by taking stock of how intolerable the situation in the Middle East is becoming. Iran is asserting control over the Strait of Hormuz, while surging inflation threatens to send shockwaves through the global supply chain. Tehran believes it can withstand TrumpвАЩs blockade longer than he can withstand its grip over the flow of energy. The missing piece of this story is just how dire conditions have become inside Iran, with massive unemployment and the hollowing out of the middle class. How long can the regime survive under these conditions?  In the second half of the show, Rudyard and Janice turn to TrumpвАЩs visit to China this week and Xi JinpingвАЩs striking invocation of the вАЬThucydides TrapвАЭ to warn of the dangers of escalating strategic rivalry between the U.S. and China. Yet despite the symbolism and spectacle, the visit produced no major breakthroughs or concrete agreements. Meanwhile, European leaders and much of the Global South are increasingly viewing Xi as a more predictable and stable actor amid the constant disruption and volatility associated with Donald Trump. arts.title: Michael Frayn
arts.summary: Over a seven-decade career, Michael Frayn has been acclaimed as a novelist, playwright, journalist, translator & memoirist. From his comedies вАУ including the stage farce Noises Off, and a screenplay for Clockwise starring John Cleese, and the novels Headlong and Skios вАУ to the complex political, historical and scientific themes of his stage plays Democracy and Copenhagen, he has been prolific in a diverse array of genres and subjects. arts.summary: A literary mystery came to me via a meme: вАЬSomeone translated Dracula into Icelandic, and it took over 100 years for anyone to point out he just made a fanfic rewrite of what he wanted the story to be.вАЭ  In this first instalment of a short series about three versions of Dracula, we familiarise ourselves with the plots of Dracula published by Bram Stoker in 1897; the Icelandic version Makt Myrkranna by newspaper editor Valdimar √Бsmundsson, serialised in his newspaper Fyallkonan in 1900-1901; and the Swedish version M√ґrkrets makter by an author known only as AвАФe, and serialised in a couple of Swedish newspapers 1899-1900. arts.title: Venice Biennale special: Lubaina Himid
arts.summary: TODAY on the GWA Podcast: a very special bonus episode with Lubaina Himid, on her British Council Commission for the British Pavilion at Venice Biennale 2026, "Presenting History: Testing Translation". Returning to the podcast for the second time, Lubaina Himid is an acclaimed Lancashire-based artist working in paintings, sculpture, installations, archives, and more, whose career spans from the 1980s вАУ when she was established as one of the leaders and trailblazers of Britain's Black Arts movement вАУ to the 2020s, where she uses her art and her training in theatre to create all-encompassing works that tackle silenced histories. While you can go to episode 33 to hear a deep dive into Himid's life and work, today we are focusing on her pavilion for Great Britain at the 61st Venice Biennale! An event that happens every two years, thought of as the most important space to showcase art and artists, the Venice Biennale revolves around a central exhibition, this year curated by the late Koyo Kouoh, titled "In Minor Keys", and is often a signifier to define not just what is happening in the world, but how we can understand it through art. The rest of the Biennale is made up of pavilions вАУ think of it like the Olympics of art, whereby countries have exhibition spaces, nominating an artist to stage a show to compete for the gold medal equivalent, The Golden Lion.  For the British Pavilion this year Himid will showcase large multi-panel paintings drenched in her signature vibrant palette. In conversation with the British Pavilion's neoclassical architecture, the installation  will present Britain as welcoming and airy, brimming with potential, albeit with an underlying sense of unease as the texts, images, and soundscape (made in collaboration with artist Magda Stawarska) subtly introduce tension. And I can't wait to find out more.  Lubaina Himid's British Council Commission for the British Pavilion at the Venice Biennale will run from the 9th of May to the 22nd of November, 2026, and is curated by Ese Onojeruo.

arts.title: Anne Fadiman on Essays, Personal and Historical
arts.podcast_name: The World in Time / LaphamвАЩs Quarterly
arts.summary: вАЬAn 1833 review of the only book of poetry Hartley Coleridge published in his lifetime praised the verse for embodying вАШno trivial inheritance of his father's genius,вАЩ but also observed, вАШIt is an old saying that the oakling withers beneath the shadow of the oak.вАЩ I have long been interested in what makes some oaklings thrive and others wither, because, in a minor way, IвАЩm an oakling myself. My father was a critic and an essayist. My mother was a war correspondent. The upside of this print-smudged parentage was that I was raised in a home with seven thousand books, plenty of literary conversation, and empirical evidence that writing was something you could actually do for a living." ¬† This week on the podcast, Donovan Hohn speaks with essayist Anne Fadiman about the history of the essay since Montaigne and the art of making essays out of history; about teaching essay-writing to college students; and about what Virginia Woolf called вАЬthe Common Reader.вАЭ The conversation addresses the title essayвАФabout a pet frog named BunkyвАФof FadimanвАЩs new collection, Frog and Other Essays, but Hohn and Fadiman spend the most time with two other essaysвАФone that originally appeared in the Family issue of LaphamвАЩs Quarterly, on poet and essayist Hartley Coleridge, who grew up in the shadow of his famous father, Samuel Taylor, and one first published in the Harvard Review, about the South Polar Times, a magazine whose editors, contributors, and original readers were all members of the polar expeditions led by Robert Falcon Scott.
